zotero.abstractNote: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great promise in solving complex control tasks. However, learning directly on physical systems is often time-consuming, dangerous, and requires constant supervision. Consequently, training in simulation and transferring the resulting policy to the real world is a common paradigm. Unfortunately, the performance in simulation is often not predictive of the performance in reality due to the reality gap. In this paper, we propose a framework to assess the transferability of a policy from simulation to reality. We introduce a measure of transferability based on the discrepancy between the transition dynamics in simulation and reality. We show that this measure can be used to predict the performance drop when transferring a policy. Furthermore, we demonstrate how this measure can be used to guide the design of simulation environments and to select policies that are robust to the reality gap. We evaluate our approach on a variety of robotic tasks, including a cart-pole system and a robotic arm, and show that it outperforms existing methods for assessing transferability.
